You really ought to help them. But it's even worse at a distance through media, through copy, because we can't get a read. So just like I can say, I'm seeing kind of an older gentleman and I don't see any response in his eyes, I can say, no, no, no, no, I think it's an older lady, his eyes flicker a little. Boom. Right on, right? Well, salespeople can do the same thing, right? So if I start to answer your objection one way and you are not responding to me, I can shift gears and start to answer your objection another way. And maybe the third time I finally get it right, that will be okay in most one-on-one selling environments. When I have to do it at a distance in media, there's no feedback loop. The feedback loop is either you keep reading and buy or you don't, and I don't have an opportunity to do anything about it. So it is even more important to diagnose, to deduce, to try and understand what the real objection is, not the objection they would necessarily say to you if they were sitting across from you one-to-one. Because more often than not, people express their resistance differently than what their resistance really is. So the classic example when I work with Miracle here is with transcripts of these sales presentations and at now the money discussion, this very older, this guy's 70s. When he's hit with the price, which is roughly eight grand if you put one in both ears, he says, gee, I wasn't planning on spending that much money on myself right now. The salesman goes into a price value answer. So he takes this as a price objection and he is now answering it with how the shit's made in the U.S. Well, first of all, yeah, I know, it's expensive, but this is the start of his answer. The guy didn't say anything about it being expensive. He expressed no opinion whatsoever about that. So the salesman immediately makes it worse. Yeah, I know it's expensive, but we could have lived without the whole but. You know, then he's into justifying the price. It's made in the USA. It's made out of palladium. It's got 46 patents. It's guaranteed. He's justifying the price. Right then he went, the guy said, the guy said, what I said about the sweater. He said, I'm 72 years old. I got grandkids. My kids are morons. I'm worried about the grandkids having enough money to get to college. I'm saving every penny. I don't really want to spend any money on me. I'm one step away from death anyway. And you know, if I can't hear, I can just turn the TV up. It's not a big, you know, that's what he's saying. And what he's looking for is somebody to make it okay for him to spend this money on himself. What he was plaintively saying is, I'd really like to hear. That's why I came down here. Right? But I'm not going to feel good about taking eight grand out of the grandkids college fund in the bottom drawer of my dresser. I'm going to feel guilty forevermore unless you help me with this. Help me. That's what he's really saying. And so the guy completely bungles this. Not technically, if you took his objection answer out of context, other than starting with agreeing that it's expensive, the rest of the answer was like technically okay. This is just the wrong answer. And so this happens all the time. In every, if you've got salespeople, they're doing it like all the time. And the more they have gotten good at an answer to a, so they are. If all you got is a hammer, every problem is a nail. So if you got a salesperson who's really good at handling price objections, he's going to make a lot of crap in the price objections that aren't price objections. If he's really bad at empathy, he's going to answer every objection as if it was a price objection. Conversely, if he's really good at everything's a humanistic, emotional problem, he's going to take a price objection and turn it into some kind of giant emotional traumatic thing. And he's going to be talking to them about their inner child when what they really wanted to hear was how come this thing's eight grand? So it's important to figure out. So first of all, go make a list of all the objections. What would be stated, objections in your world to getting people to do whatever it is you are trying to get them to do. Then give some thought to what the possible true meanings of the objection might be. And make sure in your copy you are raising and answering, but now you got to do all the possible real objectives one way or another. Then you got to go back now to where should they be. So a lot of people clump them. That's like, I mean there's a reason to do it, but it's usually done because the copywriter's lazy. So they will usually do a frequently asked questions and answers thing all clumped together at the end of a sales presentation. That's what they'll usually do. If you understand that we're dealing with salesmanship in print when we're dealing with media, that's not how objections take place in a human give and take. They get raised along the way. There may be some raised at the very end when the guy finally talks, but they get raised along the way. They're part of the conversation. The guy doesn't hold them all. And then at the end, okay, he's got a list of seven. And then you answer them. That's not how selling works. Therefore, it's not how selling in media should work either. So most people are lazy about it and they're answering the wrong objections. I gave you a copy example from the orthodontist two-stuff, the author of the book, The Book You Can Look At It On Your Own. I got number six. This relates to the sequential agreement, sequential submission thing we talked about yesterday. And part of what that is about, part of what the 11 agreements example I gave you is about stopping people from disqualifying themselves, which is what almost everybody is doing while they are reading copy, watching a video sales letter, watching a webcast. Again, if you go back to the 11 agreements, it's relatively easy to get people past the first three or four. That yeah, it's possible and yeah, it's attainable and yeah, it would be good for a lot of people. But then they start to disqualify themselves. Yeah, for all those other people, but not for me because. So they will start, they'll essentially let the proposition slide. Okay, you can get rich and secure your retirement outside your dental practice by doing smart things with real estate. Okay, I get it, right? But then they will start to disqualify themselves, not the proposition themselves. I can't do it because I got five practices and I'm running from one office to the other office all day and every day by four o'clock in the afternoon, I'm just gasping for breath and I gotta go home and lay on the floor until tomorrow morning. So I can't even look on the computer at a listing. So they will start to, I'm shitty at math, I can't do math, I could never do this because I can't do math. So that's how everybody disqualifies themselves from doing a lot of their own marketing. I can't write. I can't write a grocery list. I flunked English, I could never do an essay. So none of this now can work for me because I can't do math. Right? So they will start to disqualify themselves. Now again, what most of us do as salespeople or marketers or copywriters is we defend the proposition. So we're worried about them not accepting the premise of the proposition. The thing we are insisting they can do. And we neglect the personal disqualification. They actually get ahead of us. We're still arguing the merits of what it is we want them to do. They already accepted that. They're arguing with themselves about themselves. So like courses. So if you go back to my magnetic marketing speech, I raise a fact that most would choose to do a lot of their own marketing. A fact that most would choose to hope just didn't occur to anybody. And that fact is you've come to a zillion seminars before and you have taken a crapload of notes and you never looked at the notes again. And you have bought a bunch of courses and you have taken them home and you have put them on a shelf and you have never taken the shrink wrap off. Well you would think if you're selling a binder of notes with shrink wrap on it, you might not want to raise that issue. But you have to because they all got there. Most of them got there before I finished talking about what's in the notebook. They already said to themselves, okay, so think of everything that had to happen. I have borrowed authority because I'm on a stage in front of 25,000 people who paid to be there. I've been with Zig and President Bush and the CEO of this. I've got all kinds of authority. Two of the speakers before me who had authority said you got to stay in here, Kennedy, because he's got the greatest shit since sliced bread. So I've got all that going for me. I've addressed problems they have and don't like and agitated them. I've been funny, they kind of like me. So I've got all that. So they've said essentially to themselves, okay, I don't really thoroughly understand it, but whatever the hell is in that notebook works. They're there. Here's where they stop and they say to themselves, yeah, but I got a shelf full of notebooks that I didn't do shit with. I know me, I ain't gonna do shit with this either. That's where they stopped. So if you don't deal with their disqualifying of themselves, you leave out half the story. Half the story is about them. And by the way, it doesn't matter how successful they are. They could be doing a million dollars a month. And if they go to seminars, they took a bunch of notes they haven't used. And they bought a bunch of stuff they haven't taken to shrink wrap up. We know this for a fact. We validated every once in a while. So we just, oh, and I'm supposed to remind you, you guys are asking me about the, they're asking Vicki about the CD set from the media summit that I mentioned. There's order forms out there now. And so the first 20 or so that got shipped, only the first of the CDs was actually duplicated with sound. All the rest of it was blank. And so we had one person called. Now 27, because we're nice guys, he went in to overdrive to fix this problem and send out new ones. At 2700, the math really suggests just wait for them to call. Because 2600, I mean, you'll never hear from. You'll never have to fix this problem. And these are not unsuccessful people. We're all guilty, right? I mean, so if you don't take that disqualification away, if you're selling opportunity to the great unwashed masses, and you've got a room full of people down the hall here that you're going to sell Get Rich in real estate to, and that's one of the ways they disqualify themselves. Because I've been in 15 other things and I never got rich. In part, because I never did shit. And I'm not going to do shit now. And they're going to go home and the spouse is going to say, you did what? You're done shit with the other 16 things you got out there. He's got to be able to tell himself and her why this time this is going to be different. And then if you go back to the magnetic marketing speech, there's a bunch of reasons why this time this is going to be different. I can't do the whole thing anymore without notes. It's been too long. But I can do one of them. And that is, these are fill-in-the-blank letters. In some cases, all you've got to be able to do is put your business's name in the right place. You don't have to think. In fact, it would be better if you didn't. You can take them home, fill in the blank, get it in the mail. The guy says, well, before I had to think. This time will be different because I don't have to think. Great. See, that ain't in there by accident. That's in there because of this. So here's four magic words. On page 32, this is the single add-on. This is the bridge to stopping disqualification. No matter what your circumstances are, your history is, no matter what. And so I think I gave you on 33 is still one of the best all-time ads in its category. It's done by a guy named Ross Jeffries. Contemporary, it's probably one of the 50 best direct response ads ever in contemporary times, definitely in its category. And it has the no matter, so here's the amazing seduction secrets of this improbable guy that you would think could never get any action. And they'll work for you no matter what your looks or age. So the main qualification somebody does. And a mistake that a lot of the how to do well with women gurus make is they show their pictures and they're pretty good looking. And guys immediately go, yeah, but I'm 68 and I'm ugly. This ain't gonna work for me. So this no matter what statement immediately addresses the main way these prospects instantly disqualify themselves. And without it there, readership of the ad would be a lot less. Project seven is reverse reality. So you have to create a different reality for something when it is one of these things. If it is too familiar, then it is fundamentally uninteresting. It's not been there, done that, another one of those. Or it's too scary to them. Or it's too incredible. It's too unbelievable. It's too demanding. Too much work. It's going to take too much time, etc. Or it's too complicated to understand. If it is any one of those things, most people will instantly reject it. Just as if I get a box of something home from Best Buy and I take the instructions out and it's more than like one side of a page, it's in a trash. Why is that? It looks like you just bought that. Why is that in a trash? Look at the instructions. I'll do it for you. I don't want you doing it for me. I'm just pissed off that I'm not going to do it and it's in the trash. It's too complicated to understand. Screw up. Which is why we don't have a DDR, we don't have Netflix, we don't have Twitter. So if it's any of these things, a bunch of people will instantly reject it. Keyword, instant. Because remember, everybody's system for buying is not to buy. Everybody's system for action is procrastination. That's what everybody's system is. About everything. You go into a store, even if you went there hoping to find something and the clerk says, can I help you? What do you say? No thanks, I'm just looking. Why the hell do you say that when you went there to get something? Because your ingrained system is to do nothing and buy nothing. So instant happens easily. By the way, if you have retail stores and you let your people use that script, you should be shocked. Because we know what the reaction is going to be to that script. We know. Nine out of ten people are going to say, no thanks, I'm just looking. So run some kind of experiment to find a better script. I'm sure Ari can give you a better script. Because that script don't work. People do it at car dealerships. You know, the poor kid waiting in the ups line, that comes in, can I show you some cars? No, no, no, I just want to look around. Wrong opening line. So there'll be instant rejection. If it can be, he will. So somehow, excuse me, third day, we have to change the reality of what it is if it is any of those things. I'll show you an example from what I'm working on for Jeff and then I'm going to show you some controls. So page 35, just take a quick look at this. In fact, actually, we'd that whole page for a second. All right, enough. Can anybody tell me what it is? You ever got a prayer telling me what that is? And I have reversed its reality. So its reality is, you the doc are being put in a cell A diabetes treatment program business in a diabetes treatment in a box, which is then administered by their back office, not by you. That's what this is. And you are actually buying the kits at wholesale and reselling them at retail. I've reversed the whole reality. We will pay you for the use of your list. That's because you're going to send all the marketing ship we give you to your list. It's all based on internal marketing. I've reversed the whole reality. It sounds like we're paying you actually you're paying you because you're buying something from X and you're selling it for Y. We're going to pay you for the use of your list. Actually, you're paying you for the use of your list. I've reversed what it is and thoroughly disguised it. And I go quite a ways before there's evolved in the lead generation piece. I never fix it. The reverse reality stays intact. The first time I start to unwind this perception. The first time I start to climb out of the hole I have dug for myself is in the full length long presentation they've requested. By the way, I'm perfectly willing often to dig a very deep hole and climb back out of it when I have to reverse the reality. So I'll show you a couple of controls. These are now going on eight to nine years with each one of them. This is a check mailer. It's personalized to the recipient. The check shows through the window on the envelope. So this is attention person's name. Part time finders are needed in. That's the person's town. You can earn finders fees of $3,000, $5,000, even $15,000 by doing some simple research on your computer. And then a bunch of this is about how you're going to get real checks and they're going to come to you by FedEx and you'll be excited. And this is all about the finders fees business. There is no reveal of what this really is on the entire first page. The second page, which you have as 37. There's no reveal on the second page. The artificial reality I have created remains in place until you actually get to the website. The reality is this is get rich in real estate. The reality is this is finding properties that can be bought out of foreclosure or from highly motivated sellers at prices so deeply discounted that you can flip them to wholesale investors. And there's a website after you. So you find them on the computer and then when you get them, you put them on the computer and you can do it all sitting on your asset home. But the reality is this is to get rich in real estate. Because once you find it, between the time you find it and you put it on the computer to unload it, you got to buy it. And you're going to own it. And if you don't unload it right away, by the way, you might want to put a tenant in it. And some number of them you might want to keep and on and on and on and on and on. This not only works with general opportunity, C4less, this thing killed with dead real estate investors. So this worked in 2008 when all the get rich in real estate guys folded up their tents and went into hibernation. All the teams were off the roads. No but webcast stopped. It all died. We took their list, ran this against it and did fine. Here's another version of it. It starts on your page 38. This is a self-mailer. So right side up is the self-mailer sign. How to earn $3,000, $5,000, $10,000 in one afternoon to simply find certain things, houses that are hidden in plain sight. The back of that is how to create a secret second income almost overnight. You can start tomorrow in your spare time. No reveal. Your page 39 is the first interior page. There's no reveal. This is a tweaked version of the same finder's fee pitch. What you do, you find it and you get it and somebody buys it from you and you earn the fee. The big part left out is that it's houses and you got to buy them because that's pretty scary. Think of everything that's wrong with that. It's scary. There's a lot of been there done that. I already got a course on the wall with the shrink wrap still on it. So I've wiped all of that out of my lead generation in one fell swoop by changing the reality of what I'm talking about because they don't have a finder's fee course. And getting finder's fees for looking something up on a computer doesn't sound very scary. If they can't do it, they got a 12 year old. It's about time that kid earned his key. Right? So there's no reveal anywhere on this page either. Your next page, which is 40, there's still no reveal. Now there's a bunch of stuff from yesterday's list all in here. If you want to play the game when you go home, you can find a bunch of them. We have to explain why, for example, and it's right here, why there's these rich investors who will let you make so much money by just finding something for them that presumably they could have found for themselves. We got to explain that. Right? So in case you haven't noticed, rich people are surprisingly lazy. They have other people do everything for them, even walk their dogs for them. In New York City, Central Park is full of professional dog walkers. It's unbelievable. Also, the rich investors I'm talking about are busy. I say, okay, I get that. Rich people are lazy bastards. So okay, I'll take their money. No reveal. 41. There's a three step wealthy finder system. There's just three steps. Find it, post it online, get paid. And you do it on a website, we give you. You don't have to go set up the website. It's set up for you. There's no reveal. I don't start crawling out of this hole until they come to the video. That's when I start crawling out of the hole. This is not in your manual, but this is another one of the control pieces. And there's something particularly interesting about this control piece. So this control piece is actually a rip off of a Harvey Brody campaign, by the way. So this is, how would you like to make, we tested like 20 headlines. This is the winner. How would you like to make money in big chunks fast? And in front of this looks like this. Big money in big chunks. Same thing, there's no reveal. I dig a hole about what it is.
